coredata.dc:title: Green-synthesized chitosan‑carbon dot nanocomposite as turn-on aptasensor for detection and quantification of Leishmania infantum parasite

coredata.dc:description: Leishmania is one of the most common diseases between human and animals, caused by Leishmania infantum parasite. Here, we have developed an ultra-selective turn-on fluorescent probe based on an aptamer and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite. The CD used in this study were synthesized using Quercus cap extract and a microwave-assisted approach. The Chitosan-CD nanocomposite was optimized using several microscopic and spectroscopic techniques to possess a bright fluorescence emission before adding aptamer and totally quenched fluorescence after addition of aptamer. The designed probe was proficient in the detection and quantification Leishmania infantum parasite by selective targeting of poly(A) binding protein (PABP) on the surface of the parasite. The designed fluorescent biosensor with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 94 cells/mL of the Leishmania infantum parasite as well as a linear response in the ranges of 188–750 cells/mL and 3000–6000 cells/mL (R2 ≥ 0.98 for both linear ranges). Additionally, the selectivity of the designed probe was evaluated in the presence of different pathogenic species such as Trypanosoma brucei parasite and Staphylococcus aureus bacteria, as well as LiIF2α and LiP2a and BSA proteins as interference substances. The results of this study shows that using Chitosan-CD nanocomposite is a great strategy for developing selective turn-on probes with extraordinary accuracy and sensitivity in identifying Leishmania infantum parasite, especially in the early stages of the disease, and it is promising for the future clinical applications.

The excitation wavelength and emission wavelength were λex = 360 nm and λem = 410 nm, respectively. The incubation time was 35 min and the final volume of the solution remained constant at 2500 mL.

head.ce:title: Green-synthesized chitosan‑carbon dot nanocomposite as turn-on aptasensor for detection and quantification of Leishmania infantum parasite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Leishmania is one of the most common diseases between human and animals, caused by Leishmania infantum parasite. Here, we have developed an ultra-selective turn-on fluorescent probe based on an aptamer and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite. The CD used in this study were synthesized using Quercus cap extract and a microwave-assisted approach. The Chitosan-CD nanocomposite was optimized using several microscopic and spectroscopic techniques to possess a bright fluorescence emission before adding aptamer and totally quenched fluorescence after addition of aptamer. The designed probe was proficient in the detection and quantification Leishmania infantum parasite by selective targeting of poly(A) binding protein (PABP) on the surface of the parasite. The designed fluorescent biosensor with high sensitivity, excellent selectivity, and a limit of detection (LOD) of 94 cells/mL of the Leishmania infantum parasite as well as a linear response in the ranges of 188–750 cells/mL and 3000–6000 cells/mL (R2 ≥ 0.98 for both linear ranges). Additionally, the selectivity of the designed probe was evaluated in the presence of different pathogenic species such as Trypanosoma brucei parasite and Staphylococcus aureus bacteria, as well as LiIF2α and LiP2a and BSA proteins as interference substances. The results of this study shows that using Chitosan-CD nanocomposite is a great strategy for developing selective turn-on probes with extraordinary accuracy and sensitivity in identifying Leishmania infantum parasite, especially in the early stages of the disease, and it is promising for the future clinical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Leishmaniasis is one of the parasitic infectious diseases found in both humans and animals [1–3]. Leishmania infantum is caused by an unwanted intracellular protozoan eukaryote from the family of trypanosomatids. Leishmania protozoa was first identified by Leishman and Donvan [4–6]. Different types of Leishmania infantum cause a wide range of diseases, from self-limiting skin ulcer disease to visceral disease or fatal black fever, especially in children as approximately half of the afflicted individuals impacted by the disease [7]. The lack of a human vaccine, increasing drug resistance and serious side effects indicate the need for research on developing new methods for detection of leishmaniasis [8].

Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) molecular methods [9,10] and immunological parasitological methods such as direct smear and culture [11,12], microscopy, serology, and ELISA [13] are laboratory techniques used to diagnose and identify the causative agent of leishmaniasis. These methods involve sampling smears of skin lesions, bone marrow, spleen, blood, and mass culture of parasites. However, they are labor-intensive, time-consuming, and costly. Among the new methods in the identification of Leishmania infantum, the utilization of aptamers has emerged as a potent technique offering notable selectivity and a propensity to bind to the desired molecule owing to their unique tertiary structure [14–16]. “Aptamers could be employed in designing fluorescent biosensors, leading to designing fast and sensitive biosensors”. [17–19] “The fluorescent aptasensors have shown promising results in diagnosis different diseases”, [20,21] which are important for clinical applications.

“Carbon dots (CDs) are a new class of nanomaterials in the scale of 2 to 10 nm with tunable optical properties, high fluorescence intensity, diverse synthesis methods, biocompatibility, water solubility, low production cost, and low photobleaching, which are used in the design of optical biosensors [22–25]. Since the structure of CDs is made of carbon atoms, they are non-toxic compared to inorganic quantum dots (i.e., which probably leave toxic metal ion residues after decomposition). These unique properties have led to the use of CDs in medicine for diagnosis and imaging applications. [26,27]

There are two main strategies for the synthesis of CDs. The top-down method that achieves small CD by exfoliating macromolecules with the methods of electron beam lithography, X-ray lithography, arc discharge, chemical oxidation, and so on. Another method is the bottom-up, where CDs are synthesized from precursors such as small molecules and carbonizing them under specific conditions, and using external energy sources such as ultrasonic, hydrothermal, and microwave [28,29].

The fluorescence emission of CD is influenced by the environment and is dependent on the interaction between the surface functional groups of the CD and the solvent. In particular, the fluorescence intensity of CD decreases in water due to interaction between CO groups on surface of CD and water molecules through hydrogen bonds. These hydrogen bonds quench the excited state in CD, leading to weaker fluorescence intensity. [30] In order to minimize the negative effective of solvent on fluorescence of CD, loading CD into hydrogel has been found as a great approach. [31]

Hydrogels based on biological macromolecules such as chitosan not only provide optical stability for CD but also enables easy interaction of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite with recognition elements such as antibody and aptamer for designing turn-on probes [32,33]. Furthermore, the incorporation of a gel framework within the nanocomposite hydrogel serves two important functions: enhancing the overall cohesion of the hydrogel and effectively preventing the accumulation of nanoparticles, thus mitigating potential fluorescence quenching. [34–36]

In the last decade, CD-based fluorescent biosensors have opened a new perspective in detection of biologically important molecules as well as live cells (human cells, plant cells, Fungai, bacteria, and viruses). The presence of many functional groups (i.e., carboxylic group, amine group, etc.) on the surface of CD has made it possible to functionalize them with various organic, polymeric, inorganic, or biological materials. The surface functionalization of CD with recognition elements (i.e., antibody, aptamer, etc.) has led them to be used in designing sensitive and selective biosensors for detection of different analytes such as metal ions, anions, organic substances, RNA, DNA, and proteins. Moreover, to the best of our knowledge, there is a no report so far based on the use of CD for the detection of Leishmania infantum parasite.

“In this study, for the first time, we report the green synthesis of carbon dots using extract of Oak cap, which is the waste part of Oak fruit. In this approach, we employed microwave as energy source to convert the extract of Oak cap to CDs in only 8 min. The amazing approach of green synthesis, which is converting biomass and low-value materials to valuable, environmental-friendly, and practical nanoparticles has turned the eyes towards itself. The two most important categories of resources used in the green synthesis of CDs are plants and food, and biomass waste (agricultural waste, animals, etc.) [37,38]. So far, CDs have been synthesized by different methods with the help of these precursors such as plant extracts, fruits, citrus fruits, chicken feathers, fruit skin, wheat straw, etc. [39] The methods that are economical in terms of energy are always taken into consideration. Employing microwave creates homogeneous heat interaction with the electric dipoles of the molecules in a few short minutes. Molecular dipoles and water molecule dipoles align themselves with the microwave electric field, leading to heat uniform and homogeneous production in the sample with minimal energy loss. Therefore, microwave-assisted green synthesis of CDs seems to be economical and environmental-friendly approach to obtain CDs with uniform and homogeneous physico-chemical properties.”

In this research, CD synthesized by green synthesis method was synthesized from oak fruit waste (oak cap) by preparing extract from (Quercus cap) with the help of microwave method with minimal energy consumption that we were able to successfully design CD with optimized fluorescence emission in the design of aptamer-functionalized Chitosan-CD nanocomposite as a specific probe for rapid, easy and quantitative detection of Leishmania infantum parasite as a skin pathogen through bright fluorescence approach for the first time. It was observed that the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite decreased after conjugation with aptamer. However, the fluorescence intensity was restored in the presence of the target pathogen due to the higher affinity between the aptamer and poly-binding protein (PABP) on the surface of the Leishmania infantum parasite. In addition, the designed biosensor showed high selectivity towards the target pathogen in the presence of other similar pathogens. We believe that the findings of this study can open a new path for the design of biosensors for the detection of parasites at low concentrations towards clinical applications.

Chitosan (low molecular weight, deacetylation degree 75–85 %), was purchased from Acros Organics (Morris Plains, NJ, USA); Penta‑sodium tripolyphosphate (Tpp), analytical grade hydrochloric acid (HCl), acetic acid, phosphate-buffered saline (PBS), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) pellets were obtained from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). RPMI 1640 medium was purchased from Gibco, Life technologies GmbH, Germany. The ss-DNA aptamer with specific sequence of oligonucleotides (5’GCGGATGAAGACTGGTGT-40NGCCCTAAATACGAGCAAC-3′) [40] was obtained from Cosmo Genetech, South Korea. Promastigotes of Leishmania infantum, Iranian strain (MCAN/IR/07/Moheb-gh; GenBank Accession No. FJ555210), Staphylococcus aureus (ATCC25923), and Trypanosoma brucei parasites MAK65 were received from the Iranian Microbes Collection at Pasteur Institute form (Tehran, Iran). Leishmania infantum proteins (LiIF2α and LiP2a) were received from Gibco, Life technologies GmbH, Germany.

Oak fruits (Quercus) were taken from the Oak Zagros trees, Ilam Province, Iran. The caps were separated and washed with deionized water, followed by drying under sunlight. Next, the dried caps were boiled in water for 30 min. The obtained extract was then passed through Whatman No. 1 filter paper for purification. (30 mL) of the extract was placed in the beaker and heated in a microwave (700 W) for 8 min. The colour of the solution turned dark brown at this stage. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min to settle the larger particles. The supernatant was filtered with a 0.22 μm syringe filter for further purification and removing unwanted products. The as synthesized CD were dried in an oven at 70
°
C and the dried CD were kept in the darkness at 4
°
C
fridge for further use.

The first step was to make a solution of chitosan (0.5 % w/v) by dissolving 0.5 g of chitosan powder in 100 of mL acetic acid (1 % v/v) and stirring the solution on a stirrer at 1800 rpm for 2 h until obtaining a homogeneous solution. A solution of tripolyphosphate (0.1 % w/v) was prepared by dissolving 0.1 g of sodium tripolyphosphate salt in 100 mL water (pH at 8.2). To form hydrogel, tripolyphosphate anion was used as cross-linker. To do this, sodium tripolyphosphate solution was added dropwise to the chitosan solution while the mixture was stirred at 1800 rpm for 1 h. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 15000 rpm for 20 min to settle the larger particles. The supernatant was separated and stored in the refrigerator (4°C) for further studies.

In order to obtain Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, the chitosan:TPP volume ratio (X = 2:1, 3:1, 4:1 and 5:1) was first optimized in the synthesis of chitosan hydrogel. Then, 450 μL of CD (150 mg/L) was added to 1.5 mL of chitosan solution, followed by dropwise addition of TPP solution as cross-linker (chitosan:TPP volume ratio of 2:1 as the optimum ratio obtained in the first step), and the mixture was stirred with 1800 rpm. Finally, by adding PBS buffer (0.01 M, pH = 7.4), the volume of the solution was increased to 2.5 mL. The solution was stirred for another 1 h before carrying out the freeze-drying process for longer shelf life.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of aptamer to in Chitosan-CD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to optimize the condition for conjugation of aptamer with Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, the amount of aptamer and the time of conjugation were tuned. In an optimized condition, 15 μL of aptamer (20 nM) (Fig. S1) was added to mixture of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite (1950 μL) and PBS buffer (535 μL, pH = 7.4). The solution was stirred at 1800 rpm for 1 h to quench the fluorescence intensity completely.

The standard strain of Leishmania infantum (MCAN/ES/96/BCN150) was cultivated in RPMI1640 cell culture medium containing 100 units/mL penicillin, 100 μg/mL streptomycin and 10 % FBS, transfected and kept in an incubator. Temperature 25 ± 1 °C, then the parasite growth was quantitatively and qualitatively checked with daily microscopic observations to check the appearance of short and long flagella that can infect the host and the number of promastigotes in RPMI1640 medium. Parasite count daily on Neubauer lam slides within 1–9 days, their number reached the stationary phase of growth of 3 × 105 cell/mL (Fig. S2). Then, the contents of the culture flask were transferred to centrifuge tubes under sterile conditions and centrifuged for 20 min at 10,000 rpm. The supernatant was separated and the pellet was washed three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) before further use.
Other types of organisms including bacteria and parasite, were cultured to check the selectivity of the designed fluorescent probe.

Staphylococcus aureus monoclonal was transferred to 10 mL of Muller-Hinton broth (MHB) and the culture suspension was incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, it was diluted to 0.5 McFarland standard (1.5 × 108 CFU/mL) and the absorbance value was measured at 600 nm (optical density, OD600 = 0.130). They were centrifuged for a minute and washed once with double distilled water and once with PBS buffer and resuspended in PBS buffer.

Hemoflagellate parasite, Trypanosoma.brucei MAK65, was transfected into RPMI 1604 medium containing l-glutamine, 100 units/mL penicillin, 100 μg/mL streptomycin, and 10 % FBS. They incubated in a culture flask at 25 °C for 24 h. The growth of the parasite was followed quantitatively and qualitatively daily through a microscope, and after reaching the stationary phase of growth, it was centrifuged for 20 min at 10000 rpm. The supernatant solution was separated, and the precipitate was washed three times with PBS buffer at pH = 7.4 before further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and quantification of Leishmania infantum parasite
ce:section.ce:para: To detect and quantify the Leishmania infantum parasite using the designed turn-on probe, 10 μL of the cultured Leishmania infantum parasite was added to mixture of 1965 μL of solution containing aptamer-conjugated Chitosan-CD nanocomposite and 525 μL PBS and the fluorescence spectrum was recorded after 35 min incubation. The excitation and emission were similar as before (optimized for CD) and set to 360 nm and 410 nm. The volumes of all samples were kept at 2.5 mL.

The UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent model from Cary in Germany) was utilized to obtain spectra across all wavelengths. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were read by Fluorescence Spectrometer (Carry Eclipse, Agilent, USA) with a four side optically clear quartz cell of 10 mm of path length. FTIR spectra (Jasco 4700 LE FTIR spectrometer) and Hitachi S-4160 scanning electron microscope (SEM) (Tokyo, Japan) were used. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM; JEOL 2100, with an accelerating voltage of 200 kV) was used for taking images from samples. Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) Tescan, LYRA 3 XMU, Czech Republic, was used to image the nanoparticles. Dynamic Light Scattering (DLS) was carried out with Malvern Instrument.

Zetasizer Nano ZS and The Zeta potential measurement was Zetasizer Nano 90, Malvern.X-ray photoelectron spectrum was recorded using an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with (XPS, MultiLab, 2000spectrometer, Thermo electron corporation, England).

The extract obtained from Quercus cap was used as carbon source for the microwave-assisted bottom-up synthesis of CD (Fig. 1a). Microwave energy led to successful synthesis of CD with green fluorescence emission under 365 nm UV light. The as-synthesized CD absorption spectrum shows an index peak at the wavelength of 280 nm (Fig. 1b), which could be attributed to π-π* electron transition related to sp2 orbitals in aromatic CC bonds (in the core of CD), and n-π* transitions related to CO surface bonds.

These as-synthesized CD demonstrated excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence, where the maximum fluorescence intensity was observed under 360 nm excitation wavelength (λex = 360 nm) and 410 nm Emission wavelength (λem = 410 nm) (Fig. 1c). Such an excitation-dependent behavior could be due to different transitions inside the CD. [41,42] Additionally, it could be attributed to the size distribution of CD, where nanometer changes in the size of CD could affect their optical properties [43,44].

The size of the as-synthesized CD was analyzed by dynamic light scattering (DLS) and electron microscopy techniques. The TEM image shows the spherical CD with an average diameter of 2 nm (Fig. 2a), which is in agreement with the hydrodynamic size distribution data (Fig. 2b). Examining the general X-ray photoelectron spectrum (XPS) (Fig. 2c) clearly shows C1s, N1s and O1s for the green synthesized CD. C1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) displays the presence of five peaks at 283.73 eV, 284.93 eV, 258.81 eV, 287.05 eV, 287.73 eV to CC bonds, CC, CO, CN, O-C=O, respectively, indicating the existence of graphene-like carbon rings in the CD structure. [45]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 2e) is a double peak at 398.60 eV and 399.50 eV, confirming the CN bond, CN bond, to pseudo-pyrrolic N with sp2 hybridization, and pseudo-pyridinic N with sp3 hybridization, respectively, in the CD structure. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 2f) has four peaks at 531.70eV, 533.09 eV, 533.99 eV, and 534.94 eV corresponding to OH, CO, COOH, O2 bonds, which confirms the existence of carboxylic, phenolic and hydroxyl functional groups in the CD structure.

Since the smaller size and uniform size distribution of chitosan hydrogel are preferred, we first optimized the amount of TPP in the synthesis of chitosan hydrogel and then CD were incorporated into the hydrogel under the optimized conditions. Fig. S3 illustrates the hydrodynamic size distribution of chitosan hydrogel when chitosan:TPP ratio was tuned. As can be seen, 2:1 ratio led to formation of chitosan hydrogel with smaller size and uniform size distribution. Therefore, this ratio was used for further studied. Decreasing the chitosan:TPP ratio leads to the neutralization of the electrostatic repulsion between positively charged amine groups in chitosan chains and strengthens the possibility of hydrogen bonding, creating a denser network. This change results in the less swelling of nanoparticles in the aqueous solution and formation of particles with smaller size. The observed trend in this study is in agreement with literature [46,47]. The presence of the chitosan gel framework in the Chitosan-CD nanocomposite structure causes the easy interaction of the Chitosan-CD nanocomposite with the aptamer in the detection of Leishmania parasites, in addition to this nanocomposite hydrogel, it increases the overall cohesion and prevents the accumulation of nanoparticles, and also prevents the reduction of the fluorescence emission potential of CDs [48]. This hydrogel framework consists of the connection of the cationic chain of chitosan with anionic tripolyphosphate connections and the electrostatic interaction of the formation of an intertwined chain in a spherical shape (i.e., bead shape as presented in Fig. S3e).

The morphology of the chitosan hydrogel was analyzed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Fig. S4). The prepared chitosan nanoparticles are spherical, but some of them are stuck together. Considering that the solution containing nanoparticles is dried for imaging, the particles may stick together during the drying process. However, the results of investigating the morphology of hydrogel in nano size and spherical shape show agreement with DLS results (fig. S3a).

Fig. 3a illustrates the synthesis procedure of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite. The surface morphology of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite was evaluated by field emission scanning electron microscopy (Fig. 3b). The structure includes spherical particles and cavities with many wrinkles, which may be caused by the connection of CD with chitosan by the crosslinker, as well as the exit of water from the nanocomposite structure during sample preparation for FESEM study. The image also shows formation of spherical nanoparticles with size <150 nm.

To confirm the successful synthesis of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, different spectroscopic techniques were used. FTIR spectrum was recorded for CD, chitosan hydrogel, and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite (Fig. 3c). In FTIR spectrum of CD, the broad and strong peak at 3398 cm−1 is related to the stretching vibrations of OH groups. The stretching vibrations of the CH3-CH2-CH2 group appeared at a wavelength of 2940 cm−1. The 1727 cm−1 peak is related to the carboxylic acid in the structure. The peak related to the CO bond of the vinyl ether appeared in the region of 1060 cm−1 [49], and the peak appearing at 1117–1022 cm−1 clearly indicates the presence of aliphatic C-O-C, amine CN and ring CC vibrations. Additionally, a set of peaks at 921 cm−1 to 802 cm−1 of aromatic groups (fragrant) is off-screen. [50,51]. The FTIR spectrum of chitosan hydrogel shows a peak at 895 cm−1 of P-O-P stretching, associated with phosphate groups as the result of presence of TPP molecules in the hydrogel structure. The broad peak related to the bending vibration HN of amine groups appeared at 1026 cm−1 and 1632 cm−1 [52,53]. The peak at the wavelength of 3439−1 cm is related to the stretching vibrations of HO and HN groups [54].

The FTIR spectrum of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite shows similar characteristics to both CD and chitosan hydrogel. The broad and strong peak at 3402 cm−1 is related to the stretching OH group of alcohol and the amine group of the first type NH in chitosan. The common peaks at 1417 cm−1 and 1567 cm−1, which are responsible for the NO and amine groups of CD and chitosan. It was found that the free OH stretching group of the carboxylic acid in CD disappeared in the spectrum of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, indicating the interaction between the carboxylic group of CD and the oxygen group of TPP molecules. The peak at 1215.1 cm−1 could be attributed to PO bond, indicating the interaction between TPP and chitosan molecules [55].

UV–visible spectra of samples are shown in (Fig. 3d). As can be seen, a prominent peak at 220 nm and a shoulder at 300 nm were observed in the spectrum of chitosan hydrogel, which is related to the π-π*and n-π*transitions in the aromatic composition and other functional groups. The absorption peak of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite shows a more specific absorption in both the 233 nm region and the beginning of the peak shoulder from 336 nm, indicating a Bathochromic shift due to the interaction between the chitosan and TPP molecules and CD in the nanocomposite. Fluorescence spectroscopy of CD and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite revealed a partial red shift of 3 nm from 410 nm to 413 nm in the fluorescence peak and a slight decrease in its intensity under 360 nm excitation, owing to the interaction between CD and chitosan hydrogel (Fig. 3e). However, the nanocomposite is emissive enough to be used for designing fluorescent probe.

The XRD patterns of CD and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite in the range of 2θ = 10o to 70o are shown in Fig. 3f. In the X-ray diffraction pattern of carbon dot, there is a peak at 2θ = 28.2° corresponding to crystal planes (002), which could be attributed to turbostratic carbon and graphene in the structure of CD [56]. In addition, peaks at 2θ = 11.4
°
and 13.4° are related to the level of oxygen functional groups and surface oxidation [57]. The X-ray diffraction pattern for Chitosan-CD nanocomposite had two peaks at 2θ =20.1° and 23.3°, which could be attributed to hydrated chitosan crystals that have hydrogen bonds between amino and hydroxyl groups [58,59], and the presence of peaks at 2θ =11.4° and 18.8° shows ionic and cross-linking of chitosan hydrogel by TPP molecules and the presence of hydrated and amorphous crystalline structure [60]. The presence of peaks at 2θ =13.4°, 11.4°, and 28.2° in XRD patterns of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite indicates the presence of CD in the structure of the final nanocomposite.

The XPS results for Chitosan-CD -nanocomposite clearly show that it mainly contains carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and the phosphorus (Fig. 4a). C1s spectrum in (Fig. 4b) shows five deconvoluted peaks at 283.89 eV, 284.42 eV, 285.5 eV, 286.5 eV, and 288.01 eV, which are related to CH, CC, CC, C-O/C-N, O-C=O bonds, respectively. Carbon with a single bond to oxygen (CO), carbon with a double bond to oxygen (C=O), carbonates result in the π → π* transition in the structure of chitosan and carbon dot, and CN of imidazole rings and in CD confirms the bond between carbon and ammonium in chitosan structure. The O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c) showed seven deconvoluted peaks at 530.96 eV, 531.75 eV, 531.90 eV, 532.45 eV, 533.07 eV, 533.89 eV, 536.05 eV for OP, OC and C-O-C, OH, CO /O-P, COOH and H2O,O2 bonds [61]. the N1s spectrum in shows three deconvoluted peaks at 398.43 eV, 399.47 eV, and 401.39 eV to CN, CN, NH bonds, respectively (Fig. 4d). These bonds are related to pseudopyrrolic N with sp2 hybridization and pseudopyridinic N with sp3 hybridization in the CD structure. The NH peak, which is caused by amine groups in chitosan structure, confirms the presence of chitosan. The P 2p spectrum in confirms the two deconvoluted peaks at 132.38 eV and 134.03 eV are related to P-O-P bond, P-O=P in TPP tripolyphosphate bonds in Chitosan-CD -nanocomposite structure (Fig. 4e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conjugation of aptamer to Chitosan-CD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to optimize the necessary amount of aptamer to completely eliminate fluorescence in Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, a fixed amount of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite (1950 μL) with different concentrations of aptamer (0 to 25 nM) in volume (15 μL) and PBS buffer (535 μL) pH = 7.4 was mixed. Physiological pH equivalent as shown in (Fig. 5a,b)., increasing the concentration of aptamer led to fluorescence quenching in Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, which could be due to fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect [62]. The aptamer molecules interact with surface of nanocomposite via non-covalent interaction (electrostatic). The van der Waals force between nanocomposite and aptamer molecules might modulate the long chain of aptamer with ring structure, leading to interaction between aptamer and phosphate backbone, as well as NH and OH groups in nanocomposite. The results suggested that aptmer with final concentration of 15 nM in volume 15 μL was enough for quenching the fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite. Therefore, this value was selected for further investigations. The extent of this blackout was analyzed by the Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. 5c).
(1)
I 0
/ I = 1 +
K SV
φ
= 1 +
K q
τ 0
φ
where, I0 is the initial fluorescence intensity in the absence of the quencher (aptamer), I is the fluorescence intensity in the presence of the quenching agent,
φ
is the concentration of the quencher, τ0 is the fluorescence lifetime constant of Balmer's ester quenching rate, and the graph of Io/I versus
φ
is a straight line with slope of the Ksv. Ksv was found about 0.336 L.mol.s−1, indicating that FRET is possible reason for the observed fluorescence quenching.

In order to study the required time for interaction between aptamer and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite, fluorescence quenching was monitored at different time intervals (fig. S1a,b). It was found that after 30 min, the fluorescence intensity quenched and reached a constant value. Therefore, 30 min was considered as optimum time for conjugation of aptamer with Chitosan-CD nanocomposite.

After the successful synthesis of Chitosan-CD aptamer nanocomposite, the corresponding relative fluorescence intensity of the nanocomposite biosensor in the presence of the aptamer as a (negative control) and without the presence of the aptamer as a (positive control) (Fig. S5) was considered for further investigations.

The zeta potential (ζ-potential) data obtained from different samples are shown in Fig. S1c. The as-synthesized CDs had zeta potential of −12.15 mV due to COOH and OH functional groups on surface of CDs [62]. The Chitosan-CD nanoparticles had positive ζ-potential value (+28.24 mV) indicating that CDs were incorporated inside the chitosan hydrogel and the amine functional groups in chitosan could suppress the effect COOH and OH functional groups in CD. In fact, the with negative surface charge of CDs were neutralized with chitosan through hydrogen bonding and electrostatics, and in total, placing the chitosan protective cover created a positive surface charge for the nanocomposite. On the other hand, the decrease in the ζ-potential values after addition of aptamer (from +28.24 mV to +21.17 mV) indicates a good electrostatic interaction between aptamer and Chitosan-CD nanocomposite [63].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection and quantification of Leishmania infantum parasite
ce:section.ce:para:
“Chitosan is a cationic polymer with
NH 3 +
functional groups, which was cross-linked with polyanionic agent (TPP), created a hydrogel and cohesive structure. This structure protects the CDs from the effects of environmental changes such as solvents and aggregation on their optical properties. CDs are connected to chitosan hydrogel with their surface functional groups by creating a hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interaction. Since the functional groups (OH, NH, COO) on the CD surface have different energy levels that can lead to the generation of a group of emission traps, CDs could emit bright light at 410 nm when they are excited by a light beam of 360 nm. Addition of aptamer to the solution of nanocomposite leads to quenching of fluorescence in Chitosan-CD nanocomposite through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism due to non-covalent (electrostatic) interaction between aptamer and phosphate backbone as well as NH groups in nanocomposite. In the presence of Leishmania infantum parasite, the PABP protein on the surface and cytoplasm of Leishmania infantum parasite [64] has high affinity to aptamer due to the affinity between the amino acid strands of the protein structure and the hydrogen bond between the aptamer and the PABP protein, leading to separation of aptamer from surface of hydrogel and recovery of the fluorescence in hydrogel (Fig. 6a).”

“Next, to identify the Leishmania infantum parasite by Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite, 10 μL of Leishmania infantum parasite (3000 cells/mL) was added to Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite (1965 μL) and mixed with PBS buffer (at pH =7.4, Physiological pH) to reach to 2500 μL in total. The mixture was incubated and the fluorescence signal (λex = 360 nm and λem = 410 nm) was monitored every 7 min (Fig. 6b,c).”

After 35 min, the fluorescence intensity was completely restored and the intensity remained constant with the increase of time, and 35 min was declared as the optimal time for the detection of Leishmania infantum parasites.

In order to check the detection power of the Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite biosensor in different numbers of parasites in the buffer medium, successive dilutions were performed and, in each batch, a specific number form (50–6000 cells/mL) of Leishmania infantum parasites was tested. (Fig. 6d), The results show a linear range (188–750 cells/mL with six data points at low concentrations and 3000–6000 cells/mL with four data points at high concentrations) for the recovered fluorescence intensity versus the Leishmania infantum parasite concentration. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be about 94 cells/mL for Leishmania infantum, key findings from multiple studies regarding diagnosis Leishmania infantum parasite was investigated with different sensors and their reports are collected in Table 1 . The results show that the proposed method with Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite is comparable to other methods. On the other hand, in most of the works, DNA or specific protein is extracted from the parasite and then it is targeted to determine the quantity of parasites. The obvious point in this work is that we have directly targeted the Leishmania infantum parasite as an analyte without spending a lot of time and money. It was easily identified and quantified with high detection power.

To evaluate the selectivity of Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite (Fig. 6e) from Trypanosoma brucei, Staphylococcus aureus bacteria, bovine serum albumin protein (BSA) and Leishmania infantum proteins (ribosomal protein LiP2a and translation initiation factor LiIF2α) were investigated took [65,66]. The reason for choosing Trypanosoma brucei is its high similarity with the parasite Leishmania infantum, which is transmitted to humans through mosquito bites, and in the first week of infection, the area of the mosquito bite becomes red, painful and swollen, and the parasites enter the blood and increase in the lymph nodes. and causes fever, headache, sweating and swelling of lymph nodes [67,68]. Therefore, infection with Leishmania infantum parasite may be diagnosed in the early stages due to symptoms similar to sleeping sickness also, skin infection caused by Leishmania infantum parasite is usually associated with the presence of Staphylococcus aureus bacteria [69,70].

BSA has also often been used as a model for other serum albumin proteins, particularly human serum albumin, which is 76 % structurally homologous. [71] therefore, these bacteria may interfere with the results. Since the aptamer sequence specifically identifies the PABP protein on the surface of the Leishmania infantum parasite and prevents false results, other ribosomal proteins and translation initiation were considered as potential interfering substances for the selectivity tests. The fluorescence of Chitosan-CD nanocomposite was recovered only in the presence of the target analyte (Leishmania infantum parasite), which can be attributed to the aptamer used in this study due to the completely specific selectivity with the target molecule (PABP protein) on the Leishmania infantum parasite surface. This Chitosan-CD-aptamer nanocomposite can be used in the development of new diagnostic tools for Leishmania infantum, especially for health care centers.

Finally, to investigate the specificity of the selected aptamer in this work, we compared three different selective aptamers presented by Guerra-Pérez et al. [40], which are shown in Fig. S6a-c. As can be seen in Fig. S6d, the selected aptamer in current research (ApPABP#3) led to higher fluorescence intensity due to its higher selectivity towards Leishmania infantum parasite. These results are in agreement with disassociation constant values reported by Guerra-Pérez et al. [40].

In the present study, for the first time, we have prepared an environmentally friendly nanocomposite using chitosan and green synthesized CDs obtained by microwave-assisted reduction of Quercus cap extract. The as-synthesized hydrogel nanocomposite was functionalized with specific aptamer which was selective towards PABP protein on surface of Leishmania infantum parasite. The as-synthesized nanocomposite showed emission at 410 nm, when it was excited at 360 nm. The fluorescence intensity of nanocomposite was successfully quenched in the presence of aptamer, leading to be potentially used as a turn-on probe for detection and quantification of Leishmania infantum parasite. The designed probe was an innovation in nanocomposite-based optical biosensors, which had golden advantages such as simplicity and high sensitivity towards Leishmania infantum parasite. The designed turn-on probe showed extraordinary sensitivity by producing a strong CD fluorescence signal in the nanocomposite structure in the presence of Leishmania infantum parasite, leading to detection limit of 94 cells/mL, which is the lowest LOD value reported compared to literature (see Table 1). Additionally, this approach was simple (i.e., without the need for sample purification) and had fast response. The findings of this study could be used for ambiguous applications such as analysis of blood and serum samples of suspected people who feel are bitten by insects and have observed initial symptoms or skin lesions of the pathogen in their blood or serum samples. Such an innovation could lead to identification of false negatives, leading to a better treatment plan for suspected patients at the early stages to prevent the spread of infection.
